text: Two ports are connected to the robotic arms: one for the robotic endoscope and another available for surgical assistance, facilitating the robot-assisted minimally invasive splenectomy.
The surgeon mobilizes the left colonic flexure by sectioning the gastrocolic ligament, providing access to the lesser sac.
The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation to treat the short gastric vessels, followed by sealing and sectioning them with ultrasonic shears.
The surgeon dissects the main trunk of the splenic artery, placing a vascular loop around the artery to prevent direct manipulation of the vessel.
The surgeon uses an endoscopic vascular clamp to close the splenic artery, reducing the risk of bleeding and decreasing the spleen's volume during the section.
The surgeon identifies and preserves two branches supplying the lower pole of the spleen, while the trunk vascularizing the upper pole is clipped using a vascular clamp.
The surgeon removes the spleen and dissects the main trunk of the splenic vein, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
A vascular loop is placed around the splenic vein to facilitate its dissection, while the tail of the pancreas is completely detached from the ascending part of the splenic hilum.
The surgeon clips and transects the identified upper pole artery of the spleen, completing the vascular control of this region.
The surgeon clips and sections the superior branch of the splenic vein, completing the dissection of this vascular structure.
The surgeon frees the superior transected branches of the splenic hilum from the upper surface of the pancreatic tail, ensuring complete separation of these structures.
The surgeon identifies the demarcation line after completing the devascularization of the upper pole and proceeds with the parenchymal transection using ultrasonic shears.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps to achieve hemostasis on the transected splenic parenchyma, ensuring control of bleeding at the surgical site.
The surgeon sections the splenoprenic ligaments, fully freeing the transected spleen from surrounding attachments.
The surgeon removes the vascular loops from the surgical field, ensuring the area is clear following the previous vessel ligation and transection steps.

The surgeon places the transected spleen into a retrieval bag for extraction through a small suprapubic incision. The splenophenic ligament is reconstructed to prevent torsion of the remaining spleen on its vascular pedicle.

The surgeon applies a hemostatic matrix to the dissected splenic parenchyma, ensuring hemostasis. A small drainage tube is visibly placed near the surgical site, intended to remain for twenty-four hours before removal.
